name: God/Allah (spiritual)
shortDescription: A monotheistic deity recognized across Abrahamic religions, representing the supreme being and ultimate source of creation, morality, and judgment.
description: God/Allah represents the singular, omnipotent, omniscient, and omnipresent deity worshipped in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. While the specific interpretations and traditions vary across these faiths, the core concept remains consistent: a transcendent being responsible for the creation and sustenance of the universe, the establishment of moral law, and the ultimate judge of humankind. Attributes commonly associated with God/Allah include mercy, justice, wisdom, and love. The manifestation of God/Allah is primarily spiritual, though divine intervention and communication may occur through prophets, scriptures, and miracles. The relationship between humanity and God/Allah is understood as one of devotion, obedience, and seeking guidance. Different theological schools within each religion offer varying perspectives on the nature and attributes of God/Allah, leading to diverse interpretations of divine will and the path to salvation or enlightenment. The name 'Allah' is specifically used in Islam, while 'God' is the common term in English-speaking Christian and Jewish contexts, although the underlying concept refers to the same ultimate being.
appearance: No physical form; a purely spiritual entity.

sources.title: The Holy Quran
sources.author: Various scribes, traditionally attributed to divine revelation

sources.title: The Hebrew Bible
sources.author: Various scribes, traditionally attributed to divine inspiration

sources.title: The New Testament
sources.author: Various authors, traditionally attributed to divine inspiration

worshipPractices.name: Prayer
worshipPractices.description: Prayer is a fundamental practice involving communication with God/Allah, often through spoken or silent words. It serves as a means to express gratitude, seek guidance, offer repentance, and petition for blessings, fostering a personal connection with the divine.

worshipPractices.name: Charity and Almsgiving
worshipPractices.description: Giving to the poor and needy is considered an act of worship, reflecting God/Allah's compassion and promoting social justice. This practice emphasizes empathy, generosity, and the importance of caring for those less fortunate.

worshipPractices.name: Sacrifice
worshipPractices.description: While interpretations vary, sacrifice, both literal and symbolic, can be a form of worship. It represents dedication, devotion, and the willingness to offer something valuable to God/Allah, acknowledging His supreme authority.

festivals.name: Passover/Easter/Eid al-Adha
festivals.timing: Varies annually based on religious calendars
festivals.description: These festivals commemorate significant events in each faith's history, often involving themes of sacrifice, redemption, and divine intervention. They typically include special prayers, feasts, and acts of charity, reinforcing communal identity and faith.

festivals.name: Christmas/Eid al-Fitr
festivals.description: Christmas celebrates the birth of Jesus, involving gift-giving, family gatherings, and religious services. Eid al-Fitr marks the end of Ramadan, the month of fasting, with celebratory prayers, feasts, and charitable giving.

festivals.name: Rosh Hashanah/Yom Kippur
festivals.description: Rosh Hashanah is the Jewish New Year, a time for reflection and repentance. Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, is a day of fasting and prayer, seeking forgiveness for sins committed during the past year.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of God/Allah continues to shape ethical frameworks, moral values, and social justice movements globally. It also inspires countless works of art, literature, and music, influencing philosophical debates and spiritual quests in diverse cultures and communities, and providing a framework for understanding life's purpose.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Brahman represents the ultimate, unchanging reality and source of all existence, similar to the concept of God/Allah as the origin of creation and the underlying principle of the universe.
The Tao is the fundamental and ineffable principle underlying all reality, akin to God/Allah as the ultimate source and guiding force behind the universe, though less anthropomorphic.

While not a single god, the concept of Fate in Greek mythology embodies an overarching, inescapable power that dictates the course of events, paralleling God/Allah's role in predestination and divine will.
During Akhenaten's reign, Aten was worshipped as the singular, supreme god, the source of all life and light, resembling the monotheistic nature and creative power attributed to God/Allah.

associations.name: Lion
associations.significance: The lion symbolizes power, kingship, and divine authority, often associated with rulers who are seen as representatives of God on Earth.

associations.name: Light
associations.significance: Light represents divine illumination, knowledge, and the dispelling of darkness (ignorance or evil), mirroring God's role as the source of all understanding.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: The sun is a symbol of life-giving energy, warmth, and the ultimate source of light, reflecting God's role as the sustainer of creation.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: White symbolizes purity, holiness, and divine grace, representing God's unblemished and perfect nature.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: The number one represents unity, singularity, and the indivisible nature of God as the sole creator and ruler of the universe.

associations.name: Eternity
associations.significance: Eternity symbolizes God's timeless existence, transcending the limitations of beginning and end.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents both purification and divine power, symbolizing God's ability to cleanse and judge.

companions.name: Archangel Gabriel
companions.description: Gabriel is a messenger of God, often delivering divine revelations and instructions. He serves as a key intermediary between the divine and human realms.

companions.name: The Holy Spirit
companions.description: In Christian theology, the Holy Spirit is the third person of the Trinity, co-equal with God the Father and God the Son. It is considered the active presence of God in the world and a source of guidance and inspiration.

companions.name: Prophets (Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Muhammad)
companions.description: These figures are considered chosen messengers of God, entrusted with conveying divine teachings to humanity. They serve as examples of faith and obedience to God's will.

companions.name: Angels
companions.description: Angels are celestial beings who serve God and carry out his commands. They act as intermediaries between God and humans, offering protection, guidance, and assistance.

companions.name: Sufi Saints
companions.description: In Sufism, saints are individuals who have achieved a high level of spiritual realization and closeness to God. They are considered exemplars of divine love and wisdom.

extendedContent.title: Origin and Nature of the Divine: A Comparative Look
extendedContent.content: Across the Abrahamic traditions, God/Allah is understood as the uncreated creator, existing eternally and independent of the universe. The Hebrew Bible (Tanakh) begins with Genesis, portraying God as bringing order from chaos through divine speech, 'Let there be light' (Genesis 1:3). This act of creation ex nihilo, from nothing, establishes God's absolute power and transcendence. Similarly, the Quran describes Allah as the 'Creator of the heavens and the earth' (Quran 6:101), emphasizing His unique role in initiating existence. While both texts share a creation narrative, nuanced differences exist. The Christian concept of the Trinity introduces a complexity absent in Judaism and Islam, positing God as Father, Son (Jesus Christ), and Holy Spirit – three distinct persons unified in one divine being. This doctrine, solidified in the Nicene Creed, sets Christianity apart, while Judaism and Islam maintain a strict monotheism, emphasizing the absolute oneness (Tawhid) of God/Allah. These variations highlight different theological interpretations of the divine nature within the Abrahamic family of faiths. The inherent unknowability and mystery surrounding God/Allah are acknowledged across all three religions, fostering both reverence and theological debate.

extendedContent.title: Encounters in Myth: Prophecy, Revelation, and Divine Intervention
extendedContent.content: Encounters with God/Allah in mythology often take the form of prophecy, revelation, and divine intervention. In the Hebrew Bible, prophets like Moses receive divine law (the Ten Commandments) directly from God on Mount Sinai (Exodus 20), establishing a covenant between God and the Israelites. This encounter underscores God's active involvement in human history and His willingness to communicate His will. In Christianity, the incarnation of Jesus Christ represents the ultimate divine encounter, with God becoming human to redeem humanity from sin. The Gospels detail numerous miracles performed by Jesus, demonstrating God's power and compassion. In Islam, the Quran is believed to be the literal word of Allah, revealed to the Prophet Muhammad through the Angel Gabriel (Jibril). This revelation, recorded in the Quran, serves as the ultimate guide for Muslims and emphasizes the importance of submission (Islam) to Allah's will. These encounters, while different in their specific manifestations, share a common theme: God/Allah actively engages with humanity, providing guidance, redemption, and a path to spiritual understanding. The narratives surrounding these encounters shape the moral and ethical frameworks of each respective faith.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Meaning: Attributes and Representations of the Divine
extendedContent.content: God/Allah, being transcendent and beyond human comprehension, is often represented through symbolic attributes rather than physical depictions. In Judaism, God is often referred to as Adonai (Lord) or Yahweh, names considered too sacred to be uttered aloud by some. The Ark of the Covenant, containing the tablets of the Ten Commandments, symbolizes God's presence and covenant with the Israelites. In Christianity, symbols like the cross represent Christ's sacrifice and redemption, while the dove symbolizes the Holy Spirit. The fish (Ichthys) was an early Christian symbol used to identify believers. In Islam, Allah is often represented by the name itself, written in elegant Arabic calligraphy. The crescent moon and star are commonly associated with Islam, though their origins are more cultural than strictly theological. The 99 names of Allah, each representing a divine attribute (e.g., the Merciful, the Compassionate, the Just), provide a deeper understanding of Allah's nature. These symbols and attributes serve to convey the power, mercy, and justice of God/Allah, offering a means for believers to connect with the divine on a deeper level. They serve as reminders of the divine presence in everyday life and inspire devotion and reverence.

extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance: Shaping Morality, Law, and Society
extendedContent.content: The concept of God/Allah has profoundly shaped the morality, law, and societal structures of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. The Ten Commandments, received by Moses, form the bedrock of Jewish and Christian moral law, emphasizing principles such as honoring parents, abstaining from murder, theft, and adultery. These commandments have influenced legal systems and ethical frameworks across Western civilization. Similarly, Islamic law (Sharia) is derived from the Quran and the Sunnah (the teachings and practices of Prophet Muhammad), providing guidance on all aspects of life, from prayer and fasting to family law and financial transactions. The belief in divine judgment, a common thread across all three religions, reinforces moral accountability and encourages righteous behavior. The concept of charity (Tzedakah in Judaism, Agape in Christianity, Zakat in Islam) is central to each faith, promoting social justice and compassion for the less fortunate. Religious institutions, such as synagogues, churches, and mosques, serve as centers of community, providing spiritual guidance, education, and social support. The belief in God/Allah, therefore, extends far beyond individual faith, shaping cultural norms, legal systems, and the very fabric of society.

subtitle: The singular deity in Abrahamic religions: Judaism, Christianity, Islam.
